blurb: Sky-blue, tissue-paper flowers open at sunrise and drop by noon — a long-season, drought-tough Colorado native that reseeds into airy, naturalistic drifts.
spread: Self-sows readily; not invasive — volunteers are welcome and easy to thin
seasons: Delicate sky-blue blooms May–Jul, with fresh flowers opening each morning; buff seed capsules ripen Jul–Aug; fine blue-green stems remain upright into fall
wildlife: Nectar for native bees and butterflies; seeds taken by birds and small mammals in fall and winter; larval host for some blues (Lycaenidae)
deer: Generally deer-resistant due to tough, fibrous stems

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 3 and native up to ~9,500 ft in Colorado — it shrugs off Front Range winters without protection. Choose Linum lewisii (the native species) over the look-alike Linum perenne, which is a non-native introduced European species.
care.planting: Easiest planted by direct seeding in fall (Oct–Nov) so winter cold stratifies the seed naturally and it germinates with snowmelt in spring. Container-grown transplants can go in the ground in spring (Apr–early May) once frost danger is past, or in early fall (Sep); water in well but little supplemental water is needed after the first season. Pots dry out fast and need more frequent watering than in-ground plants; blue flax is not a typical container subject.
care.sun: Full sun (6+ hours) is essential. It grows lank and blooms poorly in part shade and is prone to lodging in rich, moist, shaded spots.
care.soil: Lean, sharply drained soil is the key to success — sandy loam, gravelly soil, or a droughty clay-loam all work. Blue flax does not like constantly wet or heavily amended soil. It tolerates a broad pH range including the alkaline clay-loam typical of Front Range yards (pH roughly 6.0–8.0), but will rot in waterlogged winter ground.
care.water: Essentially xeric once established — natural precipitation (~15–18 in/yr on the Front Range) is adequate after the first season. Keep the seedbed or new transplants evenly moist through establishment (a few weeks); after that, deep, infrequent supplemental watering only in the hottest, driest stretches of summer. Rich, moist soil causes floppy, pest-prone plants.
care.spacing: Space transplants or thin seedlings to 12–18 in apart; each plant stays narrow (roughly 1 ft wide). For meadow or naturalistic plantings, seed generously in drifts and thin to 12 in for the first season — the colony fills in over time via self-sowing.
care.propagation: Direct seed is the easiest method. Sow in fall (preferred) for natural cold-stratification, or in very early spring. For spring sowing, mix seed with damp sand and refrigerate for 4–6 weeks before sowing. Press seed shallowly into firm, weed-free soil — a bare-soil covering of about ¼ in is ideal; it needs warmth and some light. Germination is erratic, 1–3 weeks with temperature cycling. Cuttings are not practical (deep taproot); division is generally not recommended as the taproot dislikes disturbance. Let plants self-sow for a self-sustaining colony.
care.bloom: May through July on the Front Range, with the peak flush in June. Each sky-blue flower lasts only a morning, but a mature plant carries dozens of buds and keeps opening fresh flowers daily across a 6–8-week season.
care.feeding: None. Blue flax is a lean-soil native — fertilizing makes it lanky, floppy, and short-lived. Skip compost and amendments in the planting area.
care.maintenance: Cut back to a few inches after seed ripens in late summer (Aug) to keep plants tidy and encourage reblooming; or leave the seed capsules for birds and self-sowing. Replace plants every 2–3 years as they are somewhat short-lived; self-sown seedlings take over naturally. Thin spring volunteers to the spacing you want.
care.selfsow: Self-sows freely enough to maintain a permanent colony in open, disturbed, or gravelly soil. Seedlings are easy to spot and pull while small; thin rather than eliminate them. Not listed as a CO noxious or invasive species.
care.troubles: Essentially trouble-free on well-drained sites. Aphids occasionally cluster on new growth; blast off with water. The main failure mode is wet, heavy soil or overwatering — plants yellow, lodge, and rot. Short plant life (2–4 years) is normal; let self-sown replacements take over.
